answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Orderly (1.5°C-aligned) — NGFS orderly 'net-zero 2050' / IPCC SSP1-1.9 / CCC 'Tailwinds'
Disorderly (other-aligned) — NGFS disorderly 'delayed 2°C' / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / CCC 'Headwinds'
Hot-house world (>3°C-aligned) — NGFS hot-house 'current policies' / IPCC SSP5-8.5 / CCC current policies
answers.evidence: "Orderly (1.5⁰C-aligned) ... Disorderly (other-aligned) ... Hot-house world (>3⁰C-aligned) ... Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 ... Global transition and energy use: NGFS orderly 'net-zero 2050' scenario / NGFS disorderly 'delayed 2°C' / NGFS hot-house 'current policies' ... New Zealand transition pathways: Climate Change Commission (CCC) 'Tailwinds' / CCC 'Headwinds' / CCC current policies"
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Core Banking Group's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.34–35
answers.notes: Three entity-specific scenarios blending IPCC SSP, NGFS, and NZ Climate Change Commission (CCC) pathways. NIWA RCP projections also used for NZ physical parameters.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: ~1.4°C (Orderly/1.5°C-aligned scenario — noted as the closest available IPCC scenario to 1.5°C), ~1.8°C (Disorderly scenario), and ~4.4°C (Hot-house world scenario). No explicit temperature overshoot language is used, but the document notes the 1.5°C scenario actually projects 1.4°C due to limited scenario availability.
answers.evidence: "Approximate warming at 2100: ~1.4°C [Orderly] / ~1.8°C [Disorderly] / ~4.4°C [Hot-house world] ... It should be noted that, due to the limited number of scenarios available and the nature of temperature projections being based on probabilities and ranges, this has led to our 1.5°C scenario having a projected temperature in 2100 of 1.4°C (which is the closest available IPCC scenario to 1.5°C)."
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Core Banking Group's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.34–35
answers.notes: The document explicitly states the 1.5°C-aligned scenario projects 1.4°C at 2100 due to data availability constraints.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: (1) NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System) — for transition data and the three overarching scenario categories; (2) IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) — for physical/SSP data; (3) NIWA (National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research) — for New Zealand regional physical parameters; (4) CCC (Climate Change Commission / He Pou a Rangi) — for New Zealand transition pathways.
answers.evidence: "For physical data, we have used the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSP) scenarios. For transition data, we have used the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios." and "New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 8.5" and "New Zealand transition pathways: Climate Change Commission (CCC) 'Tailwinds' / CCC 'Headwinds' / CCC current policies"
answers.location_hint: p.33 (scenario development) and p.34–35 (scenario architecture table)
answers.notes: All four providers are explicitly named in the document.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 ... New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 8.5"
answers.location_hint: Architecture of the Core Banking Group's Three Entity-Specific Scenarios, p.34–35
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (SSP1-1.9, SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5) for global parameters and RCP identifiers (RCP 2.6, RCP 8.5) for New Zealand physical parameters, combining them across the same scenarios. These are from different scenario families (IPCC AR6 SSPs vs. IPCC AR5 RCPs).

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "Therefore, our own scenarios have been developed, as described below, through blending recognised and coherent scenarios on physical and transition risks together, incorporating relevant datapoints and elements. Narrative detail has then been added based on those datapoints and relevant information for our business." and "Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)."
answers.location_hint: p.33 (scenario development rationale) and p.58–59 (scenario analysis datapoints)
answers.notes: The scenarios are explicitly described as entity-specific, developed by blending IPCC SSP, NGFS, NIWA regional, and CCC NZ-specific data. NIWA regional downscaled data is used for NZ physical parameters. The document also notes "Down-scaled data not available under scenario" for the Orderly scenario, confirming downscaling was attempted/used where available.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "The scenario development analysis and results involved engagement and governance at a number of levels of the business: Project leads (CSO and Senior Risk Advisor) – led and coordinated work and material creation. Subject matter experts (SMEs) – provided specialist input as required. A steering committee consisting of the Chief Risk Officer, Chief Financial Officer, General Manager Country Banking and project leads – provided direction, input and oversight. Executive committee approvals (via the Risk Management Committee or Leadership Team meetings) – these challenged and provided feedback and recommended approvals to the Board. Board – reviewed, discussed and approved scenario architecture. The Core Banking Group worked with consultancy Deloitte to assist with scenario development and assess the related Climate-Related Risks and Opportunities."
answers.notes: The scenario development was internal (with Deloitte as consultant). No language such as "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", or "stakeholder input" from external parties is used. The process was governance-driven internally.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described against which the three climate scenarios are compared. However, the Hot-house world scenario (NGFS "current policies") implicitly represents a no-additional-policy baseline, and the heatmap methodology uses a "worst-case scenario" as its reference point.
answers.evidence: "To identify exposures, we have chosen to rely on a worst-case scenario for climate change using various trajectories for Greenhouse Gas concentrations spanning a period up to 2100. From a physical risk perspective, using a worst-case scenario better serves discussions on mitigating actions to be taken than do more favourable assumptions."
answers.location_hint: Assets Vulnerable to Physical Risks Methodology, p.64–65
answers.notes: No formally labelled "baseline" or "reference" scenario is defined. The hot-house world scenario (current policies) could be interpreted as a de facto baseline, but this is not explicitly stated as such in the document.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "Data source: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)." [Orderly Scenario] and "Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)." [Disorderly and Hot-house World Scenarios]
answers.notes: GCAM (Global Change Analysis Model) versions 5.3+ and 6.0 are explicitly named as the economic/transition data models. NIWA regional projections and IPCC WGI Interactive Atlas are used for physical data but are data sources/tools rather than named models per se.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "Acute risk factors are those related to more frequent and intense extreme climate events such as heatwaves, droughts, bushfires, floods and storms." and "Rabobank's heatmaps capture both acute and chronic events." and "This analysis process was qualitative rather than quantitative. This has the advantage of removing the need for complicated quantitative modelling and some of the limitations that approach presents such as a failure to accurately measure all impacts, especially those from acute events." and "Degraded ecosystem services, frequent storms and on-farm damage and asset loss result in widespread default and presents liquidity risk"
answers.location_hint: Physical risk section p.31; heatmap methodology p.64–65; scenario narratives p.38–39
answers.notes: Acute physical risks (floods, storms, droughts, wildfires, heatwaves) are explicitly included in scenario narratives and heatmaps. The scenario analysis is qualitative rather than quantitative for acute events, but damage/loss impacts (e.g. loan defaults, asset damage) are described in the scenario narratives.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "Intense rainfall days / Hot days / Frost days / Sea-level rise (with vertical land move) / Global GHG emissions / Global GDP / Global population / Global oil price / Global agricultural demand / Global CCS use / Global carbon price" [listed as scenario datapoints for Disorderly and Hot-house World scenarios at 2050]
answers.notes: These variables are explicitly listed in the scenario datapoint tables. The Orderly scenario lacks downscaled NZ physical data (rainfall, hot days, frost days, sea-level rise) but includes the transition variables.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "Data sources: Physical: NIWA regional, high-intensity rainfall and sea-level rise projections and IPCC WGI Interactive atlas regional synthesis. Economic: GCAM 5.3+ NGFS World Downscaled and GCAM 6.0 NGFS (GDP only)." and "Global physical and socioeconomic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP1-2.6 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 ... New Zealand physical parameters: eg. NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 2.6 / NIWA RCP 8.5"
answers.location_hint: Scenario architecture table p.34–35; Scenario Analysis Datapoints p.58–59
answers.notes: Global parameters come from IPCC SSP scenarios and GCAM/NGFS models; New Zealand/regional physical parameters come from NIWA regional projections. The document also notes "Down-scaled data not available under scenario" for the Orderly scenario's NZ physical variables, confirming downscaling was used where available.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: now–2030; Medium-term: 2030–2050; Long-term: 2050–2100. Rationales are explicitly provided for all three timeframes.
answers.evidence: "Short-term: now – 2030 – to align with the existing 5-year horizons in strategic planning, Medium-Term Planning (MTP), Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and Funding Plan. During 2023, MTP horizon was extended to 2030 for asset growth by sector to align with Sector x Country Plans. Medium-term: 2030–2050 – to reflect the contractual maturity profile of the Core Banking Group's loan book. Long-term: 2050–2100 – to recognise the intergenerational nature of farming and growing in New Zealand, ensure understanding of the longer-term implications of climate impacts and guard against the historical trend of improved science leading to impacts being brought forward."
answers.location_hint: Summary of Scenario Time Horizons and Risk Profiles, p.35
answers.notes: All three time horizons have explicit justifications provided. The document also notes these align with Rabobank's global periods (2020–2030, 2030–2040, 2040–2050) used in heatmaps and stress tests.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "In developing our scenarios, the XRB's recommendation to use sectoral scenarios where advisable was considered both for efficiency and to aid comparability. As the only specialised agribusiness-focused bank operating in New Zealand, two sets of sector scenarios were relevant to us – the New Zealand Banking Association as well as Aotearoa Circle's Agriculture Sector Climate Change Scenarios. Neither of these provided us with the scenarios that best described plausible futures for us or in our view challenged our business sufficiently. The Core Banking Group also sought to align our climate scenarios with Rabobank to facilitate better reporting and analysis at a Rabobank-wide level. Therefore, our own scenarios have been developed, as described below, through blending recognised and coherent scenarios on physical and transition risks together, incorporating relevant datapoints and elements."
answers.location_hint: Assessing Impacts and Developing Business Resilience Through Climate-Related Scenarios, p.33
answers.notes: The document explicitly justifies the scenario choice: (1) existing sectoral scenarios (NZBA and Aotearoa Circle) were considered but rejected as insufficient; (2) alignment with Rabobank global reporting was sought; (3) IPCC SSPs were chosen for physical data and NGFS for transition data. The rationale column in the scenario architecture table also provides per-scenario justifications.
